subject: Physics
solution:
The formula to calculate the power dissipated in the first case can be written as

P1=V02R   ...1

When the wire is halved, its resistance will also become half the previous value.

Thus, the formula to calculate the power dissipated in the second case can be written as

P2=V02R2×2= 4V02R   ...2

Divide equation (2) by equation (1) to calculate the required ratio.

P2P1=4V02RV02R=4=16

Hence, x=16.

subject: Physics
solution:
Let $G$ be the resistance of the galvanometer and $I_g$ be the current required for full scale deflection.

When the galvanometer is shunted with $S = 2\,\Omega$, it acts as an ammeter of range $I = 500\text{ mA} = 0.5\text{ A}$. The potential difference across the galvanometer and the shunt is equal:

$I_g G = (I - I_g) S$

$I_g G = (0.5 - I_g) \times 2$

$I_g G + 2 I_g = 1$

$I_g = \dfrac{1}{G + 2}$

When a resistance $R = 470\,\Omega$ is connected in series, it acts as a voltmeter of range $V = 10\text{ V}$. The total voltage is the sum of the voltage drops:

$V = I_g (G + R)$

$10 = I_g (G + 470)$

$I_g = \dfrac{10}{G + 470}$

Equating the two expressions for $I_g$:

$\dfrac{1}{G + 2} = \dfrac{10}{G + 470}$

$G + 470 = 10(G + 2)$

$G + 470 = 10G + 20$

$9G = 450$

$G = 50\,\Omega$

Answer: $50$

subject: Physics
solution:
Resistance of wire $(R)=\rho \frac{l}{A}$ If wire is bent in the middle then
$$
\begin{aligned}
& l^{\prime}=\frac{l}{2}, A^{\prime}=2 \mathrm{~A} \\
& \therefore \text { New resistance, } \mathrm{R}^{\prime}=\rho \frac{l^{\prime}}{\mathrm{A}^{\prime}} \\
& =\frac{\rho \frac{l}{2}}{2 A}=\frac{\rho l}{4 A}=\frac{R}{4} \text {. } \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$

solution:
We are given a galvanometer connected in the first circuit in the above figure, where E=6 V, R = 11 kΩ and the deflection θ = 9 divisions. The galvanometer has a resistance of G. The current in the circuit,

I=ER+G  ...(1)

Also, since the figure of merit is 60 μA/division

I = 60×9 μA = 540 μA  ...(2)

Substituting equation (2) in (1),

540×10-6 = 611,000+G⇒G = 6540×10-6-11,000⇒G = 111 Ω  ...(3)

In the second circuit, it is given that the deflection is half that of the previous circuit. So, current flowing through the galvanometer half the first circuit. Hence,

I2= ER+GSG+S ×SS+G    ⇒      I2=ESRS+G+GS

⇒S= RG ×I2E-(R+G)I2

Substituting the values from equations (1), (2) and (3),

S= 11 ×103 ×111 ×5402 ×10-66-62=110 Ω

subject: Physics
solution:
To convert a galvanometer to an ammeter, a shunt resistance $S$ is connected in parallel. For full-scale deflection:
Current through galvanometer: $I_g = 1$ mA
Total current: $I = 5$ mA
Current through shunt: $I_s = 5 - 1 = 4$ mA
Since voltage across both is equal: $I_g \cdot G = I_s \cdot S$
$0.001 \times 100 = 0.004 \times S$
$S = \frac{0.1}{0.004} = 25$ Ω

subject: Physics
solution:
Wheatstone bridge with P = 1Ω, Q = 2Ω, R = 2Ω, S = 4Ω
$\frac{P}{Q} = \frac{R}{S} = \frac{1}{2}$ → Bridge is balanced, no current through middle 1Ω.
$R_{AB}$: Upper (3Ω) and Lower (6Ω) in parallel = $\frac{3 \times 6}{9} = 2Ω$
Total resistance = 1 + 2 = 3Ω
Current $I = \frac{9}{3} = 3$ A
Power between A and B: $P = I^2 R_{AB} = 9 \times 2 = 18$ W
Heat in 1 min = $18 \times 60 = 1080$ J

subject: Physics
solution:
The four parallel branches between the common left node $A$ and the common right node are all identical: each consists of a $27\text{ V}$ source in series with a $3\ \Omega$ resistor. In the third branch, the two cells $(14\text{ V}$ and $13\text{ V})$ aid each other, giving a net EMF of $14 + 13 = 27\text{ V}$.

Thevenin equivalent between $A$ and $B$:
Since all four branches have the same EMF and polarity in parallel:
$V_{th} = 27\text{ V}$

The four $3\ \Omega$ resistors in parallel give:
$R_{th} = \dfrac{3}{4} = 0.75\ \Omega$

Load branch (diagonal):
The diagonal connecting $A$ and $B$ consists of two $3\ \Omega$ resistors in series, so:
$R_L = 3 + 3 = 6\ \Omega$

Current through the diagonal branch:
$I = \dfrac{V_{th}}{R_{th} + R_L} = \dfrac{27}{0.75 + 6} = \dfrac{27}{6.75} = 4\text{ A}$

Voltage across $A$ and $B$:
$V_{AB} = I \cdot R_L = 4 \times 6 = 24\text{ V}$

Hence, the voltage is $24\text{ V}$ and the current is $4\text{ A}$, which corresponds to option $(2)\ 24\text{ V},\ 4\text{ A}$.

subject: Physics
solution:
Let resistances be $R_1$ and $R_2$
So, $S=R_1+R_2$;
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{P}=\frac{\mathrm{R}_1 \mathrm{R}_2}{\mathrm{R}_1+\mathrm{R}_2} \\
& \mathrm{~S}=\mathrm{nP} \\
& \mathrm{R}_1+\mathrm{R}_2=\frac{\mathrm{nR}_1 \mathrm{R}_2}{\mathrm{R}_1+\mathrm{R}_2} \\
& \left(\mathrm{R}_1+\mathrm{R}_2\right)^2=\mathrm{nR}_1 \mathrm{R}_2
\end{aligned}
$$
If $R_1=R_2$, so minimum value of $n=4$.
